TPS.protocol_summary: NEUROLITH TPS targeting default mode network nodes; 6 sessions over 2 weeks; 6000 pulses/session at EFD 0.20 mJ/mm², 5 Hz. CE-cleared for Alzheimer's in Europe.
TPS.key_references: Beisteiner 2019 (Adv Sci, n=35, 230 citations); Radjenovic 2025 (Psychol Med, n=58); RCT JAMA Network Open 2025; Systematic Review CNS Neurosci Ther 2023; Meta-analysis 2025
TPS.side_effects: Transient headache (~4%), mood deterioration (~3%), dizziness, fatigue. 93% no AEs.
TPS.notes: 31 papers (2019–2026). 13 RCTs in overall TPS evidence base. CERAD CTS improved p=.017, d=.32. 9.8-pt gain in top-quartile. 3-month durability. Memory network upregulation fMRI. CE-cleared.

TPS.brain_target: M1, SMA, DLPFC, Cingulate Motor Area
TPS.protocol_summary: NEUROLITH TPS targeting motor and cognitive circuits; 12 sessions over 4 weeks; 10,000 pulses/session (8000 brain + 2000 foot). EFD 0.25 mJ/mm², 4 Hz.
TPS.montage: Multi-site: M1 + SMA + DLPFC + foot targets
TPS.key_references: Osou 2023 J Neurol (n=20, 20 citations); RCT 2025 – TPS Enhances Dexterity in PD (sham-controlled); EEG cortical oscillatory changes 2025; SA Perspective Brain Stimulation 2025
TPS.side_effects: Transient headache, dizziness, fatigue. Generally well tolerated.
TPS.notes: 9 papers (2021–2026). First sham-controlled RCT confirms dexterity improvement (2025). EEG oscillatory changes documented. Foot+brain combined protocol (10,000 pulses).

TPS.protocol_summary: NEUROLITH TPS broad cortical coverage; 6 sessions over 2 weeks; 6000 pulses/session.
TPS.montage: Multi-site cortical coverage (frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital)
TPS.key_references: Radjenovic 2025 (Psychol Med, 58 dementia/MCI); Nayeri 2023 Annals Clin Transl Neurol (19 citations); Biomedicines 2024 (6 citations); 24-week MCI trial 2025; Meta-analysis 2025
TPS.side_effects: Headache, mild fatigue. Very well tolerated.
TPS.notes: 7 papers (2021–2026). 24-week trial shows sustained cognitive benefit. Connectivity modulation on fMRI. No sham-controlled RCT yet for MCI specifically.

TPS.protocol_summary: NEUROLITH TPS targeting left DLPFC similar to Alzheimer's protocol. Active clinical trials ongoing (NCT05551585).
TPS.key_references: Keeser 2023 IJERPH (Pilot RCT, n=30, 34 citations); 3x RCTs CINP 2025; RCT Protocol Frontiers 2022 (5 citations); NAc TRD case 2023; Asian multicenter RCT 2025
TPS.side_effects: Transient headache, mood fluctuation. Well tolerated.
TPS.notes: 14 papers, 6 RCTs (2022–2026). Rapidly expanding. 3 new RCTs at CINP 2025. NAc target for TRD explored. Active NCT05551585 ongoing RCT.

TPS.protocol_summary: NEUROLITH TPS frontal targeting for executive function deficits. Emerging protocol.
TPS.key_references: Sham-controlled pilot RCT Frontiers Neurol 2024 (11 citations — adolescents); RCT Protocol 2023 (5 citations); Asian RCT multicenter 2025
TPS.notes: 2 RCTs (2023–2026). Pilot RCT in adolescents: significant symptom reduction. Double-blind sham-controlled design. Prefrontal targeting, 6000 pulses, 5 Hz PRF.

TPS.protocol_summary: NEUROLITH TPS targeting prefrontal and posterior parietal social networks. Emerging protocol.
TPS.key_references: Beisteiner 2023 Brain Communications (21 citations, RCT); RCT Protocol 2022 (7 citations); Brain connectivity post-hoc Autism Research 2025; Asian multicenter RCT 2025
TPS.side_effects: Well tolerated; standard TPS side effect profile.
TPS.notes: 4 RCTs/protocols (2022–2026). Strongest NIBS evidence for ASD. Network connectivity changes documented. Independent Asian cohort confirms 2025.

TPS.protocol_summary: NEUROLITH TPS targeting frontoparietal consciousness network. Emerging application.
TPS.key_references: Emerging DOC literature 2024–2025; TPS Opens New Horizons in Psychiatric Patients 2025
TPS.notes: Very early stage. Limited case reports. Frontoparietal consciousness network targeted. May complement other DOC interventions (tDCS, TMS, PBM).

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: High-frequency rTMS left DLPFC; FDA cleared; 10 Hz, 120% rMT, 3000 pulses/session, 37.5 min, 30 sessions over 6 weeks. Standard of care.
TMS_rTMS.side_effects: Headache (30%), scalp discomfort, rare seizure (<0.1%), tinnitus risk at high frequencies.
TMS_rTMS.notes: Most studied noninvasive brain stimulation intervention. Standard 6-week course. Augment with antidepressants.

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: Intermittent theta burst stimulation (iTBS); FDA cleared; 50 Hz bursts at 5 Hz carrier; 600 pulses in 3 min; non-inferior to standard rTMS in THREE-D trial.
TMS_rTMS.side_effects: Headache, scalp discomfort. Lower seizure risk than standard rTMS at short sessions.
TMS_rTMS.notes: Shorter session (3 min vs 37.5 min). Non-inferior to standard rTMS. Useful for high patient volume.

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: Deep TMS with BrainsWay H7 coil targeting medial PFC/ACC; 20 Hz; 2000 pulses/session. FDA cleared. Often paired with brief symptom provocation before stimulation.
TMS_rTMS.side_effects: Headache, scalp discomfort. Brief symptom provocation required pre-session.
TMS_rTMS.notes: Provocation protocol before stimulation enhances efficacy. Targets corticostriatal circuitry.

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: Deep TMS targeting bilateral insula and prefrontal cortex; 10 Hz; FDA cleared 2020. Targets craving circuits.
TMS_rTMS.notes: Brief script exposure before stimulation (craving induction). Significant abstinence rates vs sham.

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: High-frequency rTMS right DLPFC; 20 Hz, 2400 pulses/session. Growing evidence base.
TMS_rTMS.side_effects: Headache, scalp discomfort. Generally well tolerated.
TMS_rTMS.notes: Right DLPFC preferred by some protocols; others target L-DLPFC. Often combined with trauma-focused therapy.

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: High-frequency rTMS motor cortex/SMA; 10–25 Hz, 80–120% rMT. Reduces motor symptoms, improves gait.
TMS_rTMS.key_references: Repetitive TMS and Parkinson meta-analysis (Chou 2015); Lefaucheur 2014
TMS_rTMS.notes: Often combined with physiotherapy. TBS protocols also used.

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: iTBS left DLPFC for PD-associated depression; 600 pulses/session.
TMS_rTMS.notes: Addresses PD's high comorbid depression prevalence. iTBS preferred for tolerability.

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: Dual approach: Inhibitory 1 Hz contralesional M1 or excitatory high-freq ipsilesional M1. Paired with physical therapy.
TMS_rTMS.montage: Figure-8 coil; contralateral or ipsilateral M1
TMS_rTMS.side_effects: Headache, scalp discomfort. Low seizure risk.
TMS_rTMS.notes: Must pair with PT. Evidence strongest for upper limb motor recovery. Timing relative to stroke matters.

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: High-frequency rTMS contralateral M1; 10 Hz, 2000–3000 pulses/session. Standard pain TMS protocol.
TMS_rTMS.regulatory_status: Investigational (CE marked in EU for pain)
TMS_rTMS.notes: M1 stimulation modulates descending pain inhibitory pathways. DLPFC also targeted for affective pain component.

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: Low-frequency inhibitory rTMS (1 Hz) over left temporal auditory cortex; reduces phantom sound perception.
TMS_rTMS.side_effects: Headache, scalp discomfort. Generally very well tolerated.
TMS_rTMS.notes: 1 Hz inhibitory protocol reduces hyperactive auditory cortex. Response rate ~50%.

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: Low-frequency 1 Hz rTMS over L-TPJ to suppress hyperactive cortex generating hallucinations.
TMS_rTMS.notes: Add-on to antipsychotics. Target: TPJ/Wernicke's area. Reduces auditory verbal hallucinations.

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: High-frequency (20 Hz) rTMS left DLPFC for cognitive enhancement and neuroplasticity in AD.
TMS_rTMS.side_effects: Headache, scalp discomfort. Generally well tolerated.
TMS_rTMS.notes: Often combined with cognitive training (NeuroAD / NICE protocol targets 6 sites).

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: Low-frequency inhibitory 1 Hz rTMS to reduce cortical excitability at seizure focus.
TMS_rTMS.side_effects: Headache. Very low seizure risk if protocol followed.
TMS_rTMS.notes: Inhibitory rTMS. Requires EEG/imaging to localize focus precisely.

TMS_rTMS.protocol_summary: Single-pulse TMS (sTMS) applied to occipital cortex at migraine onset. FDA cleared (eNeura SpringTMS).
TMS_rTMS.total_sessions: As needed (up to 3 pulses at onset)
TMS_rTMS.side_effects: Mild tingling, no serious AEs in trials.
TMS_rTMS.notes: Self-administered device. Applied at onset of aura/migraine. CE marked in Europe as well.

tDCS.protocol_summary: Anodal stimulation of L-DLPFC with cathodal reference at R supraorbital; 2 mA, 20–30 min, 10–20 sessions. SELECT-TDCS and ELECT-TDCS trial protocols.
tDCS.devices: Soterix HD-tDCS, Flow Neuroscience, Newronika HDC-STIM
tDCS.side_effects: Tingling, itching, mild redness at electrode site. Occasionally headache.
tDCS.notes: Home use possible (Flow Neuroscience device). Best combined with antidepressants or psychotherapy.

tDCS.protocol_summary: Anodal M1 stimulation modulates descending pain inhibitory pathways; 2 mA, 20 min, 5–10 sessions.
tDCS.side_effects: Tingling, itching, mild skin redness. Very safe.
tDCS.notes: Works for fibromyalgia, CRPS, post-surgical pain. Evidence strongest for neuropathic and central sensitization pain.

tDCS.eeg_position: Ipsilesional C3 or C4 (Anode), contralateral Fp (Cathode)
tDCS.protocol_summary: Anodal stimulation ipsilesional M1 to promote motor recovery; paired with physical/occupational therapy.
tDCS.montage: Anode ipsilesional M1 / Cathode contralateral M1 or Fp
tDCS.notes: Timing relative to rehab session critical. Concurrent stimulation during PT is optimal.

tDCS.protocol_summary: Anodal L-DLPFC to enhance working memory and executive function; widely used in healthy aging and MCI research.
tDCS.montage: Anode F3 / Cathode Fp2 or F4
tDCS.notes: Large heterogeneity in literature. Online (during task) effects strongest.

tDCS.protocol_summary: Various montages targeting auditory cortex or DLPFC to modulate tinnitus distress and loudness.
tDCS.notes: No single optimal montage established. Multi-target approaches may be superior.

tDCS.protocol_summary: Dual-site stimulation: anodal L-DLPFC + cathodal L-TPJ to modulate prefrontal-temporal imbalance.
tDCS.montage: Anode F3 / Cathode T3 or CP5
tDCS.notes: Targets auditory verbal hallucinations and cognitive deficits. Cathodal temporal cortex reduces hallucinations.

tDCS.protocol_summary: Anodal M1 stimulation for widespread pain reduction in fibromyalgia; 2 mA, 20 min.
tDCS.notes: Also combined with aerobic exercise for enhanced outcomes.

tDCS.protocol_summary: Anodal right DLPFC stimulation for attention and inhibitory control improvement in ADHD.
tDCS.notes: Both L- and R-DLPFC protocols studied. R-DLPFC targets inhibitory control more directly.

tDCS.protocol_summary: Bilateral anodal DLPFC to reduce craving and improve executive control in addiction.
tDCS.notes: Targets craving via prefrontal top-down control of limbic system. Applicable to alcohol, cocaine, nicotine.

tDCS.protocol_summary: Cathodal stimulation to reduce cortical excitability at seizure focus; 1–2 mA.
tDCS.notes: Cathodal inhibitory protocol. Requires precise localization of epileptogenic zone.

taVNS_tVNS.protocol_summary: taVNS via left ear cymba conchae; 25 Hz, 250 μs pulse width, 0.5–2 mA, 30–60 min/day, 4–12 weeks. Activates NTS-locus coeruleus-PFC circuitry.
taVNS_tVNS.montage: Left ear clip — cymba conchae electrode
taVNS_tVNS.side_effects: Mild tingling, occasional skin irritation at ear electrode. Very safe.
taVNS_tVNS.notes: Non-invasive alternative to implanted VNS. Activates vagal afferents → NTS → brainstem neuromodulation.

taVNS_tVNS.protocol_summary: taVNS for epilepsy adjunct; 25 Hz, 250 μs, 30 min twice daily. Add-on to AEDs.
taVNS_tVNS.side_effects: Mild tingling, skin irritation. Very safe.
taVNS_tVNS.notes: Mirror of implanted VNS for epilepsy indication. Strong evidence for add-on seizure reduction.

taVNS_tVNS.protocol_summary: taVNS at higher frequency (100 Hz) for analgesic effect; 250 μs, 3 mA, 30–60 min.
taVNS_tVNS.notes: Higher frequency (100 Hz) preferred for analgesia vs 25 Hz for other indications.

taVNS_tVNS.brain_target: Cervical vagus nerve (right side) or left ear
taVNS_tVNS.protocol_summary: gammaCore cervical nVNS at migraine onset: 25 Hz, 2-min stimulation × 3 applications. Auricular versions also used for prevention.
taVNS_tVNS.regulatory_status: FDA Cleared (gammaCore — cluster headache, migraine prevention 2018–2019)
taVNS_tVNS.side_effects: Mild redness, tingling at neck. Generally very safe.
taVNS_tVNS.notes: gammaCore is FDA cleared for cluster headache (acute) and migraine prevention. Strong evidence.

taVNS_tVNS.protocol_summary: taVNS for anti-inflammatory effect; cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway. 25 Hz, ongoing trials.
taVNS_tVNS.notes: Anti-inflammatory via spleen-α7nAChR pathway. Also studied in heart failure and Crohn's.

taVNS_tVNS.protocol_summary: taVNS paired with rehabilitation exercises; 25 Hz; promotes neuroplasticity and motor recovery.
taVNS_tVNS.montage: Left ear cymba conchae + concurrent rehab
taVNS_tVNS.notes: Pairing with active motor rehab is critical for efficacy. Promotes BDNF and long-term potentiation.

taVNS_tVNS.protocol_summary: taVNS at left tragus/cymba conchae; 25 Hz. Targets auditory cortex neuroplasticity.
taVNS_tVNS.montage: Left ear tragus or cymba conchae
taVNS_tVNS.side_effects: Mild tingling, skin irritation. Very safe.
taVNS_tVNS.notes: Combined with auditory tone matching therapy for enhanced effect (paired VNS approach).

taVNS_tVNS.protocol_summary: taVNS before sleep; 25 Hz, promotes parasympathetic tone and reduces sleep onset latency.
taVNS_tVNS.notes: May work via autonomic modulation promoting sleep. Limited controlled trials.

taVNS_tVNS.protocol_summary: taVNS combined with PD rehabilitation; 25 Hz. Limited but emerging evidence.
taVNS_tVNS.notes: May reduce non-motor symptoms (depression, autonomic dysfunction). Motor data limited.

taVNS_tVNS.protocol_summary: Emerging taVNS for PTSD; 25 Hz. Modulates fear extinction via amygdala-prefrontal pathway.
taVNS_tVNS.notes: May enhance exposure-based therapy by modulating amygdala reactivity.

CES.protocol_summary: Alpha-Stim CES: 0.5 Hz modified square wave, 100–500 μA, 20–60 min/day via ear clips. Large meta-analysis effect size ES = -0.96.
CES.devices: Alpha-Stim M/AID, Fisher Wallace Stimulator, CES Ultra
CES.side_effects: Mild skin irritation, occasional headache. Extremely safe. No serious AEs in FDA review.
CES.notes: FDA cleared since 1991. Strong meta-analytic evidence. Mechanism: increases alpha EEG, modulates GABA and serotonin.

CES.protocol_summary: Alpha-Stim CES: 0.5 Hz, 100–500 μA, 20–60 min before sleep. Meta-analysis ES = -1.02 (sleep quality).
CES.side_effects: Mild skin irritation. Very rarely headache. Extremely safe.
CES.notes: Strong ES for sleep quality. Works within first session for some patients. Non-pharmacological alternative.

CES.protocol_summary: Alpha-Stim CES for mild–moderate depression; 0.5 Hz, 100–500 μA, 20–60 min/day. Meta-analysis ES = -0.69.
CES.side_effects: Mild skin irritation. Extremely safe. No serious AEs.
CES.notes: FDA cleared. Best for mild-moderate depression. Less powerful than TMS but excellent for home use.

CES.protocol_summary: Alpha-Stim with MET (microcurrent electrical therapy) probes for localized pain in addition to CES ear clips.
CES.montage: Bilateral ear clips + MET probes on pain sites
CES.notes: MET probes allow localized pain relief in addition to central CES effects. Headache, CRPS, back pain.

CES.protocol_summary: Alpha-Stim CES used in US military PTSD programs; 60 min/day. Reduces hyperarousal and anxiety.
CES.notes: Widely used in VA/DoD programs. Addresses sleep and anxiety comorbidities. Not FDA cleared specifically for PTSD.

CES.protocol_summary: CES 24-week treatment; fMRI study showing functional changes in corticostriatal circuits.
CES.notes: Very limited evidence. fMRI shows corticostriatal modulation. Requires replication.

CES.brain_target: Cortical bilateral (via ear clips) + MET probes
CES.protocol_summary: Alpha-Stim CES + MET probe application for fibromyalgia widespread pain; 60 min daily.
CES.notes: Combined approach (CES + MET probes). May reduce widespread pain and fatigue.

tACS.protocol_summary: Nexalin tACS: 15 mA, 77.5 Hz sinusoidal AC, 8-week program. Highest current FDA-compliant device for insomnia. Strong RCT evidence.
tACS.key_references: Nexalin clinical trials; Emerging RCT data 2022–2024
tACS.side_effects: Phosphenes (visual flickers, common at >1 mA), tingling. Safe within limits.
tACS.notes: Nexalin uses FDA-cleared 77.5 Hz frequency for sleep. Unique high-current protocol. Entrains sleep-related oscillations.

tACS.protocol_summary: Alpha (10 Hz) tACS entrainment of frontal alpha oscillations; or 77.5 Hz. Augments frontal alpha known to be deficient in depression.
tACS.notes: Frequency specificity key: 10 Hz alpha for depression. Can combine with tDCS for multimodal effects.

tACS.protocol_summary: 40 Hz gamma tACS targeting bilateral temporal cortex; 2 mA, 20–30 min. Promotes gamma oscillations linked to amyloid clearance (GENUS research).
tACS.key_references: Clancy 2022; Herrmann 2013; Iaccarino 2016 (40 Hz γ mouse)
tACS.notes: Strong mechanistic rationale from GENUS work. 40 Hz gamma drives microglia to clear amyloid. Multiple ongoing trials.

tACS.protocol_summary: 10 Hz tACS frontal-temporal connectivity; 2 mA. Targets disrupted fronto-temporal gamma/alpha rhythms.
tACS.notes: Targets fronto-temporal synchrony deficits in schizophrenia. Also 40 Hz gamma studied.

tACS.protocol_summary: 10 Hz tACS with AFz-Cz montage targeting prefrontal-cingulate circuitry for delusion reduction.
tACS.notes: Very limited evidence for delusions specifically. AFz-Cz targets mPFC/ACC circuit.

tACS.protocol_summary: Theta (4 Hz) tACS targeting motor areas to reduce tremor and improve gait. Low current (1–2 mA).
tACS.notes: Frequency specificity: 4 Hz (theta) for tremor modulation in PD. Beta frequency also studied.

tACS.protocol_summary: Theta/alpha tACS targeting default mode and attentional networks; 1 mA; emerging protocol.
tACS.notes: Targets theta/alpha dysregulation in ADHD. Very preliminary evidence.

tACS.protocol_summary: Various frequency tACS for chronic pain; modulates sensorimotor oscillations and descending pain control.
tACS.notes: Optimal frequency for pain not established. Alpha and gamma both studied.

tACS.protocol_summary: 1–2 mA tACS over bilateral auditory cortex to normalize aberrant oscillations in tinnitus.
tACS.notes: Targets hyperactive auditory cortex. Alpha and gamma frequencies under investigation.

PBM.protocol_summary: Vielight Neuro Gamma: 810 nm NIR, 40 Hz pulsed, DMN targeting via transcranial + intranasal, 20 min daily, 12 weeks. Targets cytochrome c oxidase in mitochondria.
PBM.devices: Vielight Neuro Gamma (810 nm + intranasal), SYMBYX PDCare
PBM.regulatory_status: Investigational (CE marked in some EU countries)
PBM.side_effects: Very minimal. Mild warmth sensation. Extremely safe profile.
PBM.notes: 810 nm: best transcranial penetration. Intranasal reaches hippocampus via cribriform plate. Pulsed 40 Hz for gamma entrainment.

PBM.protocol_summary: 810 nm NIR transcranial PBM over affected regions; continuous or pulsed; 20 min/day. Reduces neuroinflammation and promotes neuroregeneration.
PBM.montage: Transcranial LED/laser helmet or targeted probes
PBM.side_effects: Very minimal. Mild warmth. Extremely safe.
PBM.notes: Addresses mitochondrial dysfunction post-TBI. Reduces oxidative stress. Promotes ATP production.

PBM.protocol_summary: 810 nm NIR bilateral DLPFC transcranial PBM; 20 min/session. Increases regional cerebral blood flow and mitochondrial function.
PBM.side_effects: Very minimal. Mild warmth. Extremely safe.
PBM.notes: Emerging evidence. May be combined with CES or tDCS for synergistic effects.

PBM.protocol_summary: 810–1070 nm PBM: transcranial motor cortex + intranasal delivery targeting basal ganglia/substantia nigra pathway. Neuroprotective and anti-inflammatory.
PBM.devices: Vielight Neuro, SYMBYX PDCare Laser (1070 nm)
PBM.side_effects: Very minimal. Mild warmth. Extremely safe.
PBM.notes: Intranasal delivery reaches deeper structures including basal ganglia. Neuroprotective effect on dopaminergic neurons.

PBM.protocol_summary: 1064 nm NIR right PFC; pulsed; enhances working memory and executive function in healthy aging and MCI.
PBM.side_effects: Very minimal. Mild warmth. Extremely safe.
PBM.notes: 1064 nm penetrates deeper than 810 nm. Right PFC for executive function and working memory enhancement.

PBM.protocol_summary: 808 nm NIR laser (NEST trials); 10–20 J/cm² delivered within 24 h of stroke onset. Reduces infarct volume.
PBM.notes: Acute stroke neuroprotection concept. NEST-2 negative for functional outcome but NEST-3 ongoing. Challenging delivery.

PBM.protocol_summary: 810 nm NIR bilateral PFC for anxiety reduction; 20 min/session. Limited but emerging evidence.
PBM.notes: Very preliminary. Mechanism via mitochondrial support in PFC anxiety regulation circuits.

PBM.protocol_summary: 810–1064 nm transcranial PBM over pain-relevant cortical areas; also used locally over pain sites.
PBM.montage: Variable — motor cortex or pain-specific areas
PBM.notes: Transcranial PBM for chronic pain distinct from local PBM (well-established). Central mechanism via anti-inflammatory and mitochondrial effects.

PEMF.protocol_summary: PEMF: 1 Hz, 0.19–1.37 mT, 25 min twice daily. 62% reduction on depression scales. Some overlap with rTMS at low intensity.
PEMF.side_effects: Very minimal. Mild warmth, occasionally tingling. Very safe.
PEMF.notes: Low-intensity differentiation from TMS. Home use possible with consumer devices (NeoRhythm). 62% symptom reduction reported.

PEMF.protocol_summary: PEMF 1 Hz, 25 min twice daily, 15 days; doubled BDNF levels, 35% better cognition. Promotes neuroplasticity post-stroke.
PEMF.notes: Doubles BDNF and VEGF expression. 35% cognitive improvement reported. Should be combined with PT.

PEMF.protocol_summary: Low-frequency PEMF for TBI; 30 min sessions. Reduces neuroinflammation, promotes healing.
PEMF.notes: Animal models strong. Human data emerging. May reduce post-concussion symptoms.

PEMF.protocol_summary: PEMF for MS: various frequencies targeting fatigue, pain, and spasticity. 15–30 min sessions.
PEMF.notes: Addresses MS fatigue and pain most strongly. Spinal cord application for spasticity.

PEMF.protocol_summary: Low-intensity PEMF over epileptogenic focus; 45% response rate, 28% seizure cessation in some series.
PEMF.notes: 45% response rate and 28% seizure cessation in case series. Large RCT needed for confirmation.

PEMF.protocol_summary: PEMF for PD tremor reduction; motor cortex targeting; 30 min sessions.
PEMF.notes: Very limited PD-specific data. Motor cortex targeting for tremor. May complement TPS/TMS.

PEMF.protocol_summary: Theta/delta frequency PEMF to entrain sleep oscillations; 30 min before bed.
PEMF.notes: Delta/theta entrainment for sleep stages. Home use possible.

PEMF.protocol_summary: Low-frequency PEMF targeting prefrontal regions for ADHD executive function.
PEMF.notes: Very preliminary. May complement other ADHD interventions.

PEMF.protocol_summary: Alpha-frequency PEMF for addiction craving reduction; frontal targeting.
PEMF.notes: Very preliminary. Alpha PEMF for craving reduction.

PEMF.protocol_summary: PEMF for chronic pain; various frequencies (1–75 Hz); 15–30 min sessions. Good peripheral and central data.
PEMF.montage: Motor cortex and/or local pain area placement
PEMF.notes: Strong peripheral PEMF evidence for musculoskeletal pain. Central brain PEMF emerging.

LIFU_tFUS.protocol_summary: LIFU targeting right inferior frontal gyrus or DLPFC; significant mood improvement in open-label studies.
LIFU_tFUS.side_effects: Minimal. Transient discomfort, slight warming sensation. Very safe within parameters.
LIFU_tFUS.notes: Can reach any cortical or subcortical target with mm precision. Advantage over other noninvasive modalities for deep targets.

LIFU_tFUS.protocol_summary: LIFU targeting thalamic pain relay nuclei or ACC; modulates pain processing non-invasively.
LIFU_tFUS.montage: Focused transducer + neuronavigation (MRI or CT guidance)
LIFU_tFUS.key_references: Reznik 2020; Sanguinetti 2020; Tyler 2019; Lee 2016 Sci Rep (376 citations, visual cortex); Legon 2018 Sci Rep (265 citations, M1 inhibition)
LIFU_tFUS.notes: Unique ability to reach deep thalamic pain targets noninvasively. Requires imaging guidance.

LIFU_tFUS.protocol_summary: LIFU targeting caudate/ACC for OCD symptom reduction. Emerging modality targeting corticostriatal circuit.
LIFU_tFUS.key_references: Lee 2015; Kim 2018; Lee 2016 (somatosensory FUS); Kim 2023 (cortical + thalamic stimulation)
LIFU_tFUS.notes: Very early stage. Caudate/ACC are key OCD circuit nodes. LIFU can reach these without surgery.

LIFU_tFUS.protocol_summary: LIFU focused on epileptogenic zone to inhibit seizure focus. Precise mm-level targeting.
LIFU_tFUS.key_references: Fisher 2014; Deffieux 2013; Emerging LIFU epilepsy literature
LIFU_tFUS.notes: Exciting approach for pharmaco-resistant epilepsy without surgery. Requires precise focus localization.

LIFU_tFUS.protocol_summary: LIFU targeting NAc to modulate reward circuitry in addiction; very early-stage data.
LIFU_tFUS.notes: NAc is a key addiction circuit node accessible only via DBS traditionally. LIFU offers noninvasive alternative.

LIFU_tFUS.protocol_summary: LIFU/tFUS targeting VIM thalamus; inhibitory effect on tremor-generating circuits. Related to but distinct from MRgFUS ablation.
LIFU_tFUS.regulatory_status: Investigational (MRgFUS FDA cleared for ablation; low-intensity neuromodulation investigational)
LIFU_tFUS.side_effects: Minimal. Transient discomfort. Safe within FDA thermal limits.
LIFU_tFUS.notes: Distinct from high-intensity MRgFUS (ablation). Low-intensity neuromodulation mode. VIM targeting same as DBS.

LIFU_tFUS.protocol_summary: LIFU targeting DMN nodes and hippocampus to disrupt amyloid aggregates and promote neuroplasticity.
LIFU_tFUS.key_references: Legon 2014; Krishna 2017 JAMA Neurol (247 citations, systematic review); Davidson 2025 IEEE Rev Biomed Eng
LIFU_tFUS.notes: FUS can open BBB transiently (at higher power) to facilitate drug delivery. Low-intensity neuromodulation distinct.

LIFU_tFUS.protocol_summary: LIFU targeting DLPFC for 15 sessions; cognitive symptom improvement in pilot studies.
LIFU_tFUS.key_references: Beisteiner 2020; Leinenga 2015 (FUS mouse AD); JAMA Neurol review 2017; Lee 2024 Biomed Eng Lett (systematic review, 22 clinical studies)
LIFU_tFUS.notes: Very early stage. DLPFC targeting for cognitive deficits in schizophrenia.

tRNS.protocol_summary: Bilateral high-frequency tRNS (100–640 Hz) over auditory cortex; 1.5 mA, 10 min. Targets hyperactive auditory cortical generators.
tRNS.notes: High-frequency band (100–640 Hz) most effective. Acts via stochastic resonance mechanism.

tRNS.protocol_summary: tRNS over M1; 2 mA, 20 min; reduces central neuropathic pain in MS.
tRNS.notes: M1 stimulation for central pain in MS. tRNS produces more uniform cortical effects than tDCS.

tRNS.protocol_summary: tRNS over M1 to modulate corticospinal excitability and reduce motor symptoms.
tRNS.notes: Very limited data. tRNS reduces M1 excitability differently than tDCS — useful for PD motor circuit modulation.

tRNS.protocol_summary: tRNS bilateral DLPFC for negative symptom improvement in schizophrenia.
tRNS.notes: Targets prefrontal hypofrontality in schizophrenia negative symptoms.

tRNS.protocol_summary: tRNS L-DLPFC; emerging as adjunct or alternative to tDCS for depression.
tRNS.notes: Limited vs tDCS/TMS literature but growing. May offer advantages in terms of blinding (harder to distinguish from sham).

tRNS.protocol_summary: tRNS over M1 or V1 during learning; 10 min, enhances motor skill acquisition and visual perceptual learning.
tRNS.notes: Stochastic resonance enhances signal-to-noise in cortical processing. Particularly effective for learning paradigms.

tRNS.protocol_summary: tRNS targeting prefrontal and parietal areas for general cognitive enhancement.
tRNS.notes: May enhance arithmetic, memory, and general cognitive processing. Similar efficacy to tDCS with less polarity specificity.

tRNS.protocol_summary: tRNS bilateral IPL; 20 min/day, 2 weeks. Targets cognitive-sleep comorbidity in VCI. Emerging protocol.
tRNS.notes: Novel indication. IPL targeting for VCI addresses attention and memory processing deficits.

DBS.protocol_summary: Bilateral STN or GPi DBS; 130 Hz, 60–90 μs PW, 2–4 V/mA; 30–60% motor improvement, reduces dyskinesia. Gold standard surgical treatment.
DBS.devices: Medtronic Percept PC, Abbott Infinity, Boston Scientific Vercise
DBS.montage: Bilateral lead in STN or GPi; IPG subclavicular
DBS.side_effects: Infection risk (~3%), lead migration, dysarthria, eyelid apraxia, mood changes, worsening gait/balance in some. Surgical risks.
DBS.notes: Gold standard for motor-predominant PD with good levodopa response. STN allows medication reduction. GPi preferred if dyskinesia prominent.

DBS.protocol_summary: VIM thalamus DBS; highly effective for tremor. Bilateral for bilateral tremor. 130–185 Hz, 60–120 μs.
DBS.montage: Unilateral or bilateral VIM thalamic lead; IPG subclavicular
DBS.side_effects: Surgical risks, paresthesias, dysarthria at higher amplitude, tolerance effects over years.
DBS.notes: Highly effective (>80% tremor reduction). Also effective for MS tremor. Alternative: MRgFUS (non-invasive).

DBS.protocol_summary: Bilateral GPi DBS for generalized or cervical dystonia; 130 Hz, long PW (120–450 μs). Significant muscle tone improvement.
DBS.side_effects: Surgical risks, hardware complications, dysarthria. Generally well tolerated.
DBS.notes: Longer PW (120–450 μs) characteristic for dystonia vs PD (60–90 μs). Months to see full benefit.

DBS.protocol_summary: DBS VC/VS or STN for severe treatment-resistant OCD; 130 Hz. Significant Y-BOCS reduction.
DBS.montage: VC/VS or STN bilateral leads; IPG chest
DBS.side_effects: Surgical risks, mood changes (hypomania risk), stimulation-induced anxiety. Careful programming needed.
DBS.notes: VC/VS and STN both FDA HDE approved. Targets corticostriatal-thalamocortical OCD circuit nodes.

DBS.eeg_position: Deep subgenual cingulate, VC/VS, or MFB
DBS.protocol_summary: DBS for TRD targeting subgenual cingulate (SCC/Cg25), VC/VS, or medial forebrain bundle (MFB); three dosing combinations trialed.
DBS.montage: SCC or VC/VS leads; IPG chest
DBS.regulatory_status: Investigational (no FDA approval for depression yet)
DBS.side_effects: Surgical risks, mood instability, suicidality risk in early stimulation period. Careful monitoring required.
DBS.notes: Three targets studied: SCC (Cg25) — original Mayberg target; VC/VS — overlap with OCD; MFB — fastest response. No FDA approval yet.

DBS.protocol_summary: Bilateral ANT DBS; 145 Hz. SANTE trial: 40% seizure reduction at 2 years, 75% at 7 years. FDA approved 2018.
DBS.montage: Bilateral ANT thalamic leads; IPG chest
DBS.side_effects: Surgical risks, memory concerns, paresthesias, depression.
DBS.notes: SANTE trial: 40% seizure reduction at 2 years → 75% at 7 years. Second-line for focal epilepsy after failed AEDs and surgery.

DBS.protocol_summary: DBS for severe Tourette's; CM-Pf thalamus or GPi targets; 130 Hz. Significant tic reduction.
DBS.montage: CM-Pf thalamus or GPi bilateral leads
DBS.side_effects: Surgical risks, hardware complications, personality/mood changes.
DBS.notes: Two main targets: CM-Pf thalamus (more common) and GPi. Reserved for severe Tourette's refractory to medication and behavioral therapy.

DBS.protocol_summary: DBS targeting fornix (memory circuit) in Alzheimer's; experimental. Also nucleus basalis of Meynert (NBM) studied.
DBS.side_effects: Surgical risks. ADvance trial showed slower decline in some subgroups (younger patients).
DBS.notes: ADvance trial (Phase 2): mixed results overall but subgroup of younger patients showed benefit. Phase 3 ongoing.

DBS.protocol_summary: DBS NAc for severe addiction (alcohol, opioid, gambling); disrupts reward circuitry. Emerging investigational use.
DBS.side_effects: Surgical risks, mood/personality changes. Very invasive for addiction.
DBS.notes: Emerging use for severe refractory addiction. Ethical considerations important. NAc overlap with OCD VC/VS target.

brain_regions.functions: Executive function, working memory, cognitive control, mood regulation, cognitive flexibility
brain_regions.key_conditions: Depression, OCD, ADHD, Schizophrenia, Cognitive impairment, Addiction
brain_regions.modalities: TMS, tDCS, TPS, tACS, PBM, LIFU, tRNS, PEMF
brain_regions.notes: Primary TMS target for depression. L-DLPFC for depression; R-DLPFC for anxiety/PTSD

brain_regions.functions: Motor execution, voluntary movement generation, corticospinal tract origin
brain_regions.key_conditions: Parkinson's, Stroke, Chronic pain, Dystonia, MS
brain_regions.modalities: TMS, tDCS, TPS, tACS, PBM, PEMF, tRNS
brain_regions.notes: Contralateral targeting — L-M1 controls R body and vice versa. rMT measured here.

brain_regions.functions: Motor planning, movement sequencing, bimanual coordination, gait initiation
brain_regions.notes: Medial frontal location. TPS/TMS target for PD motor sequencing.

brain_regions.functions: Error monitoring, emotional regulation, conflict detection, pain modulation, attention
brain_regions.notes: Difficult to reach with surface stimulation. LIFU and deep TMS (H coils) reach better.

brain_regions.functions: Language production (Broca's area L), inhibitory control, cognitive flexibility
brain_regions.notes: Broca's area (L-IFG) critical for language. TPS targets as part of DMN network.

brain_regions.functions: Attention, spatial processing, visuospatial awareness, working memory buffer
brain_regions.notes: TPS targets as part of AD/MCI multi-site protocol. Right LPC for hemineglect.

brain_regions.functions: Auditory processing, language comprehension (Wernicke's L), memory encoding, semantic processing
brain_regions.notes: Primary target for tinnitus (inhibitory TMS) and auditory hallucinations (schizophrenia).

brain_regions.functions: Self-referential processing, episodic memory retrieval, visual-spatial imagery, consciousness
brain_regions.notes: Key default mode network hub. TPS targets as part of AD protocol.

brain_regions.functions: Decision-making, reward/punishment processing, emotion regulation, impulse control
brain_regions.notes: Important node in corticostriatal OCD and addiction circuits.

brain_regions.functions: Tactile sensation, proprioception, pain processing (somatotopic map)
brain_regions.notes: Adjacent to M1. Targeted in stroke and pain protocols.

brain_regions.functions: Memory consolidation, spatial navigation, new learning (declarative memory), fear extinction
brain_regions.modalities: LIFU, DBS, taVNS (indirect), TPS (indirect via DMN)
brain_regions.notes: Cannot be directly targeted by surface stimulation. LIFU, DBS, and intranasal PBM can reach it.

brain_regions.functions: Fear processing, emotional learning, stress response, threat detection
brain_regions.notes: Deep structure, not directly accessible by surface stimulation. LIFU can modulate.

brain_regions.functions: Sensory relay, consciousness regulation, gating of motor output, arousal, attention
brain_regions.key_conditions: Essential tremor (VIM), Pain, DOC, Epilepsy (ANT), PD (CM-Pf)
brain_regions.notes: Multiple nuclei with distinct functions: VIM (tremor), ANT (epilepsy), CM-Pf (Tourette's), MD (depression).

brain_regions.functions: Motor control (indirect pathway), behavioral inhibition, decision-making
brain_regions.notes: Primary DBS target for PD. Smaller target than GPi. Risk of dysarthria and mood effects.

brain_regions.functions: Motor output regulation (indirect and direct pathways), movement suppression/facilitation
brain_regions.notes: Larger target than STN, less dysarthria risk. Preferred for dyskinesia and dystonia.

brain_regions.functions: Reward processing, motivation, addiction, pleasure, aversion
brain_regions.notes: Core of reward system. DBS target for addiction and OCD. Overlap with VC/VS target.

brain_regions.functions: Interoception, pain integration, emotional awareness, taste, autonomic regulation, addiction
brain_regions.key_conditions: Chronic pain, Addiction, Smoking cessation, Depression
brain_regions.notes: Targeted by deep TMS for smoking cessation. LIFU can reach with precision.

brain_regions.functions: Motor coordination, balance, fine movement, procedural learning, cognitive functions (emerging)
brain_regions.notes: Cerebellar TMS increasingly studied. Modulates cerebello-thalamo-cortical circuit.

brain_regions.functions: Autonomic regulation, anti-inflammatory, neuromodulation of brainstem nuclei (NTS), parasympathetic tone
brain_regions.notes: Auricular branch of vagus nerve provides non-invasive access to vagal signaling pathway.

brain_regions.functions: Emotion regulation, depression circuitry, autonomous nervous system regulation, hedonia
brain_regions.notes: Mayberg's original DBS target for TRD. Aka Brodmann area 25. Defines depression circuit hub.

brain_regions.functions: Gait control, posture, wakefulness, REM sleep
brain_regions.notes: PPN DBS for gait freezing in PD. Complex target in brainstem. Requires skilled placement.

■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Highest Citation Paper: Beisteiner 2019 (Advanced Science) — 230 citations; n=35 multicenter, fMRI correlation
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Key Finding: CERAD CTS improved p=.017 (d=.32); 9.8-pt gain in top-quartile responders; 3-month durability; memory network upregulation on fMRI
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Top RCT Reference: JAMA Network Open RCT 2025 — Ultrasound Neuromodulation With TPS in Alzheimer Disease
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Journal (Top): JAMA Network Open / Advanced Science / Psychol Medicine
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Notes: Gold-standard evidence for TPS. CE-cleared indication. 10-session standard course. MRI-navigated protocol.

■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Highest Citation Paper: Osou 2023 (J Neurol) — 20 citations; first TPS PD retrospective n=20
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Key Finding: Motor dexterity improved in sham-controlled RCT; EEG cortical oscillatory changes documented; foot+brain combined protocol
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Top RCT Reference: TPS Enhances Dexterity in PD — Randomized Sham-Controlled Clinical Trial 2025
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Journal (Top): Journal of Neurology / CNS Neurosci Ther / Brain Stimulation
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Notes: First RCT 2025 confirms efficacy. 10,000 pulses/session (8000 brain + 2000 foot). 4 Hz PRF.

■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Key Finding: Significant cognitive improvement; brain connectivity modulation on fMRI; 24-week trial shows sustained benefit
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Top RCT Reference: 24-week TPS efficacy & safety trial in MCI (2025); Biomedicines 2024 connectivity paper
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Notes: 7 papers. Evidence gap: no sham-controlled RCT yet for MCI specifically. 6-session standard course.

■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Key Finding: Significant mood improvement in pilot RCT; Nucleus Accumbens target explored for TRD; 3 RCTs presented at CINP 2025
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Top RCT Reference: TPS for MDD — Randomized Double-Blind Sham-Controlled Pilot Trial 2025 (Int J Neuropsychopharmacol)
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Journal (Top): IJERPH / Int J Neuropsychopharmacol / Frontiers Neurology
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Notes: Rapidly expanding evidence base. 6000 pulses, L-DLPFC ± DMN. 3 separate RCTs in 2025 alone.

■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Highest Citation Paper: Frontiers Neurology 2024 — 11 citations; Adolescent pilot RCT
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Key Finding: Significant ADHD symptom reduction in adolescents; double-blind sham-controlled; well-tolerated
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Top RCT Reference: Efficacy and safety of TPS in young adolescents with ADHD — pilot RCT 2024 (Frontiers Neurol)
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Notes: 2 RCTs/protocols. Adolescent population. Prefrontal targeting. 6000 pulses, 5 Hz PRF.

■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Key Finding: Significant ASD symptom reduction; brain network connectivity changes documented; Asian RCT confirms in independent cohort
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Top RCT Reference: Effects of TPS on ASD: double-blind, randomized, sham-controlled trial — Brain Communications 2023
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Notes: 4 RCTs/protocols. Strongest NIBS evidence for ASD to date. 6000 pulses, prefrontal + parietal.

■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Key Finding: Pain reduction reported; NAc stimulation case in TRD/pain; emerging South American clinical use
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Top RCT Reference: TPS in AD, PD, and chronic pain: South American perspective (Brain Stimulation 2025)
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Notes: Very early stage for pain. LIFU/tFUS has stronger pain evidence (15+ papers). Consider TPS as adjunct.

■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Key Finding: Preliminary case data; frontoparietal consciousness network targeted
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Top RCT Reference: TPS Opens New Horizons in Psychiatric Patients 2025 (covers DOC)
■ TPS (TRANSCRANIAL PULSE STIMULATION) —.Notes: Very early stage. Limited to case reports and emerging single-arm data.

■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Highest Citation Paper: Lee 2016 (Scientific Reports) — 376 citations; first human tFUS V1 stimulation
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Key Finding: FUS sonication of V1 elicits phosphene perception and fMRI activation; EEG evoked potentials confirmed; safe profile
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Top RCT Reference: Lee et al 2016 Scientific Reports — Transcranial FUS Stimulation of Human Primary Visual Cortex
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Notes: Landmark paper. mm-level spatial selectivity confirmed. Individual variability in acoustic focus documented.

■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Highest Citation Paper: Lee 2015 (Scientific Reports) — 356 citations; image-guided FUS somatosensory
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Key Finding: Elicits tactile sensations with finger-level anatomical specificity; EEG evoked potentials; safe profile
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Top RCT Reference: Lee et al 2015 Scientific Reports — Image-Guided tFUS Stimulates Human Primary Somatosensory Cortex
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Notes: 356 citations. Neuronavigation-guided. Up to finger-level specificity. Threshold acoustic intensity identified.

■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Highest Citation Paper: Legon 2018 (Scientific Reports) — 265 citations; concurrent TMS+FUS paradigm
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Key Finding: FUS inhibits MEP amplitude; attenuates intracortical facilitation; reduces reaction time on simple tasks
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Top RCT Reference: Legon et al 2018 Scientific Reports — Transcranial FUS Neuromodulation of Human Primary Motor Cortex
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Notes: First human M1 ultrasound neuromodulation. Concurrent TMS+FUS paradigm validated.

■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Highest Citation Paper: Kim 2023 (PLOS ONE) — 18 citations; thalamic + cortical somatosensory stimulation
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Key Finding: FUS stimulation of VPL thalamus enhances resting-state functional connectivity for >1 hour; potential for long-term neuroplasticity
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Top RCT Reference: Kim et al 2023 PLOS ONE — Transcranial FUS Stimulation of Cortical and Thalamic Somatosensory Areas
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Notes: Deep target proof-of-concept. Connectivity changes lasting >1 hr post-stimulation. Neurotherapeutic potential.

■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Key Finding: Significant mood improvement in open-label studies targeting right IFG/DLPFC; unique deep-brain access advantage
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Top RCT Reference: Reznik 2020; Sanguinetti 2020 — LIFU tFUS for mood modulation
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Notes: 10+ papers. Advantage: can reach deeper than TMS. No FDA/CE approval yet for depression.

■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Highest Citation Paper: Lee 2015 (pain); Kim 2018; multiple tFUS pain reviews
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Key Finding: Noninvasive thalamic pain relay modulation; ACC targeting also studied; unique access to deep pain structures
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Top RCT Reference: Lee 2015; systematic review Lee 2024 (Biomed Eng Lett, 22 clinical studies reviewed)
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Notes: 15+ papers. Only noninvasive modality that can reach thalamus without surgery. Requires neuronavigation.

■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Highest Citation Paper: Leinenga 2015 (Sci Translat Med); Beisteiner 2020; Lee 2024 review (32 citations)
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Key Finding: FUS can transiently open BBB for amyloid drug delivery; low-intensity neuromodulation distinct from ablation; hippocampal targeting
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Top RCT Reference: Lee 2024 Biomedical Eng Letters — Review of functional neuromodulation in humans using low-intensity tFUS (22 clinical studies)
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Journal (Top): Biomedical Engineering Letters / Sci Translat Med
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Notes: Dual mechanism: neuromodulation + BBB opening for drug delivery. 22 clinical studies reviewed by Lee 2024.

■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Highest Citation Paper: Krishna 2017 (JAMA Neurol) — 247 citations; systematic review tFUS/MRgFUS
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Key Finding: VIM thalamus targeting inhibits tremor circuits; distinct from high-intensity MRgFUS ablation; neuromodulation mode
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Top RCT Reference: Krishna 2017 JAMA Neurology — Current Therapies, Challenges and Future Directions of tFUS
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Regulatory Status: Investigational (MRgFUS ablation FDA cleared; neuromodulation investigational)
■ tFUS / LIFU (LOW-INTENSITY FOCUSED ULT.Notes: Same anatomical target as DBS/MRgFUS but noninvasive neuromodulation mode. 247-citation systematic review.

■ STUDY QUALITY BREAKDOWN — TPS Evidence.Highest Citation Paper: 1,505 unique papers after deduplication from 2,994 records
